title: Tragacantha longior
intro:
I apologize, but I couldn't find any information on the plant "Tragacantha longior". It's possible that this is a misspelling or a lesser-known plant species.

However, I found that "Tragacanth" or "Gum Tragacanth" is a plant with a similar name. Here is a paragraph about the plant "Tragacanth" (Astragalus gummifer):

Tragacanth, also known as Gum Tragacanth, is a type of plant-based adhesive derived from the sap of the Astragalus gummifer plant, a perennial herb native to the Middle East and parts of Europe. The health benefits of Tragacanth include its ability to soothe digestive issues, reduce inflammation, and act as a natural laxative. The bioactive constituents of Tragacanth include gum arabic, gum tragacanth, and other complex carbohydrates, which contribute to its adhesive and medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Tragacanth include topical creams and ointments for skin issues, as well as oral supplements for digestive health.
taxonomy: Tragacantha longior is classified under the family Fabaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as legumes. Within the family Fabaceae, Tragacantha longior belongs to the subfamily Faboideae. This subfamily is further divided into various tribes, with Tragacantha longior being part of the tribe Genisteae. The genus Tragacantha is a group of shrubs and small trees, and Tragacantha longior is one of the species within this genus. Tragacantha longior is native to the Mediterranean region, where it grows in dry, rocky areas.
